I think a lot of you are not quite understanding what an action threshold really is made up of. The impact it has on a sale and how it interrelates with the fifth key on which is the pain threshold. The purpose of this training is to give you that ability by giving you a deeper insight into how the pieces fit together. And especially the last two action threshold and pain threshold. Let me give you a quick of the definitions here. Should know these now I'll reinforce action threshold represents the collective. Level of certainty that a prospect needs to be at before they feel comfortable enough. They feel good enough. They feel justified to take action. In other words, someone like myself that has a very low action threshold, right? I don't have to be at a 10, 10, 10. I don't have to be absolutely positively certain about I can be like, yeah, it seems pretty good. Right. I'm like, I'll buy it. Now, I'm not going to buy something that I think will hurt me because there's always this overarching principle that governs us of positive intent. Right. But that being said, if I'm just marginally certain that my life will be improved or simply not destroyed by this thing. And also with the good feeling and shot of endorphins I get when I just buy shit. That's how I am. That's most people are right. We love we love to buy. We hate to be sold to right. So that's my low action threshold. It's constructed. It's comprised of the sum of all the beliefs I have about making decisions about trusting people, both salespeople and just people in general. Are my best decisions made quickly or should they be made slowly? That's really what my action threshold is made of. Then on the other side of the equation, I always use my dad. I love to death. He's getting very old, very sad. Love him so much. And I'm really hoping to into my podcast this morning soon. So when you lower someone's action threshold, what you're doing is essentially, and by the way, I'm ready to start writing some fucking patterns on my father, even though he knows the straight line, it'll still work. But the point is, it's like enough is enough. When someone has a low action threshold, they move, they take action. When someone like my dad has this ultra high action threshold and an ethical salesperson comes along and lowers that action threshold. Guess what you do? You do a huge favor. You get someone something that they need. Does that make sense? So that's the action threshold. Then on this other side, because you have what's called the pain threshold. And the pain threshold means that essentially human beings, as we're built, when we're feeling severe pain or discomfort or angst, we will tend to act. And if it really is very severe, we will act. We will act even when we're not certain. If we're in pain, we'll move out of pain very quickly to try to get back into at least, if not pleasure, at least feeling no more pain. Okay. So now I want, there's a very short description of pain, but I'll dig into it in a few moments. I wanted to show you now, I want to show you this graph. I think it's really going to help you out. So this is your fulcrum. Your fulcrum. This is your beam, right? Now, when you weigh something, right? You have, let's say you have an object on this side. This is your object. You have to apply a force to this side to balance this or else it tips over, right? So imagine in a sales encounter, right? When someone enters an encounter, they always enter with preconceived notions, right? Those preconceived notions consist of reasons they should be buying now. They walk into, that's why they're probably there. There's some reasons why they should buy now, both on a logical and emotional level. So they walk in with reasons to buy now, which is probably why they clicked on your website or walked in your door, answered the phone, or we talked to you and get qualified. But on the same time, they also concerns. They have reasons not to buy now. They have reasons to buy now and reasons not to buy now. And at the beginning of the scale, of the sale, what happens is that they might, they're probably not balanced 100%. When you have a big scale like that, you might just have it maybe leaning slightly to the slightly down angle or slightly, or not even, they're almost balanced. And that represents like a four or five or a six on the certainty scale. So if someone's like at a five or a six or a four, it's around there, right? They're in the middle, they're sitting on the fence. Basically what it means, they enter the encounter with a match of reasons to buy in their own, the preconceived notions to buy and not to buy. They're basically matched. So the question is, will someone buy at this moment in time? Well, the answer is actually depends. Now let's just say, we always have positive intent. So if someone thinks it's going to hurt them, not going to help them, they're not going to buy no matter what. Let's just assume maybe it is just like a slight extra positive here, right? And these negatives are really, they're bouncy, but they believe the product will help them. But they're kind of balanced. Will they buy? The answer is maybe. I'll tell you why. Because this fulcrum represents their action threshold. Me, the position of the fulcrum represents the action threshold. Someone like myself that has a very low action threshold, what it means is my fulcrum is not in the middle. My fulcrum is over here. So what happens is every positive has massive weight to it. It has huge pressure on the scale. And every negative, well, it doesn't have quite that same impact as gravity. The force it exerts is not equal to the force this exerts. Because my fulcrum is over-excued to the left here. That's someone with a low action threshold. And the reason, and by this movie deeper here, what's happening is because of the movies I'm running, as a low action threshold die, I run the long positive movie, the short negative movie, which results in this fulcrum being closer to the left. We've been here a whole like, they're not, to my dad, those minor things. What if I don't like it? What if something goes wrong? He's running all into that. What happens is this has massive weight. Even if it's one or two negatives. And it corrupts his ability, pollutes his decision-making strategy. You get that? That's what's happening with so many very high action thresholds. So what we do with the straight line system is the first thing we do is as you move so on now, watch. So here's your straight line down here. This is your open. This is your closed. Your boundaries above and below, right? As you move so on down the straight line, from the open to the close, everything you're saying, everything you're doing, every document you hand your prospect, every website you direct them to, every utterance out of your mouth, every body language gesture, every tonal pattern you run that overlays your words, everything is all designed to essentially knock out negatives and build up positives. As you move someone down the straight line, that's what's happening. You are knocking out negatives, knocking out negatives, knocking out negatives, building up positives, building up positives. And then as you do that, what happens? Well, at some point here, you ask for the order, right? I mean, over here, right? You ask for the order. You've knocked out a bunch of negatives, and you've certainly built out some positives here, right? But does he buy? So like my father, not that much, but the point is, have you made progress? Yes or no? See, this is when people get screwed up. When you have these tougher buyers, what you're not realizing is that you're actually making massive progress as you're getting all the not-interested, let me think about it, let me call you back, bad time, yeah? All that objection hopping is simply a function of someone not being certain enough yet is still too many negatives versus positives in relation always to their action threshold. Does that make sense? All buyers, all prospects are not created equal. There's prospects like me where I don't have to have a zillion in one positive and virtually no negatives. That's not me. I could have eight positives and seven negatives. I'll say, fuck it, I'll buy. If I don't think it'll kill me, and I think it'll help me in some way. My dad, no, no, no, no, he's going to be damn sure of it. But the point is, when my father says, it sounds good, let me think about it, you've made massive progress. It's just below the water line, so to speak. It's not visible yet. But progress has most certainly been made. Okay, you get this. And then you make him step in and you future pace it by forcing him using language to run a positive movie on the upside. And what happens as a result of that, you slide this fulcrum back towards the center and boom, the scale plummets down. He goes, fine, I'll do it. That's what is happening when you lower someone's action threshold. You get it? And it's powerful fucking stuff. That pattern is magical. I can't tell you how many people I have seen like, no, no, no, no, no, I say, no, not, they say not interesting. Let me call you back. You'll get hard notes. They're like, no, no, no, no, right. They said, fine, I'll do it because you now essentially by doing that, you've in increased the pressure on the scale, pure physics here. That's how the action threshold works. And that's how that action threshold lowering pattern works. And in addition, there are other ways that we lower action threshold. When my father was younger and in his wilder days, right, you know, he was very, very particular about all of his possessions. And high action threshold people are typically like that. They value each possession. They're very fastidious. They just, you know, they buy something somewhere. They get a hold on to that thing, right? Because they don't buy a lot of things, right? You know, my father has the same salesperson for the last 30 years for everything. He's got one guy for everything. Now why is that? You know, it's funny. I met him. I liked him. And you know, he's this guy, just a smooth talker. Perhaps maybe Irv had got my father like that. Maybe. But 99.9% of the time, it wasn't the fact that someone lowered his action threshold by running a language pattern that made him buy. Rather, it was pain. He was in a position of pain. He had a severe lack in his life, a need that absolutely had to be filled. It became a must. For instance, his hair is growing out. What do you do? When your hair is too bushy and you can't go to work and his curls and what do you do? You have to use someone or else the pain, seriously, think it through. The pain of that is what? I'm going to be, you know, laugh that at work. I'll be fired if I go with my hair growing out. You have to have certain suppliers. Maybe at a rip of the shirt, you had to get a new suit somewhere. See, when there's pain present, you'll act. You have to, I don't care what your action threshold, what happens is this. When pain gets added on, nothing slides a folk from over. In fact, when real pain, when someone's in real pain, guess what? It goes to all of it. You become me. Your action threshold becomes all the way down to the left and you'll buy only with just some positive because you are in pain and when you're in true pain, you will act bottom line. Case in point of all my dad's possessions, the thing he loved most was his car, right? And of all his various suppliers and service providers, the one that he loved most was Jimmy at the Sonoco station, the local Sonoco station. I always make a joke. If Enzo Ferrari came in over my dad's free work on his car, Enzo, don't even fuck with my car. Jimmy's my guy. I'm touching my dad's car, but Jimmy at the Sonoco station. Now, did Jimmy lower his action threshold? No, I'm sure not. What happened was his car, he lives there. He has to use someone or else the pain starts to mount. Those at a certain point, well, my father might really hold off on making decisions. Someone's got to change the oil on his car. Someone's got to fix a flat tire. You get it. So what happens with these high action threshold people, they typically get their suppliers for things by adding necessity. They have to make a decision. So they make one because of pain. If he didn't fix his flat, so they'd be at a problem with the car, he goes to the local guy, at the local guy treats him well, bam, they got him forever. Because once a high action threshold person buys, that's it, they're done. That person that they bought from, to them, that's now their guy and they will never argue with this person again. They go to them and they will literally take their advice site unseen if they treat them correctly. That's how most high action threshold people get their suppliers. So I never really went through this before. I was always like, yeah, they probably load his notes. Not really that. They didn't lower his action threshold. He just had to do it. He had a better example that I want to probably use before because this is a perfect example that with his car, no one could touch his car but Jimmy, yet when we were like 13 years old, 12, 13, we were on a family trip down the East Coast. We're somewhere around Maryland of Virginia, right? And I just take to remember the car broke down. Steam came out of the radiator. Red light came flashing on. Car truck over the side. What do you think my dad did? Six o'clock at night, his family in the back seat where the God knows where, but fuck Virginia somewhere, right? You know, we just watched movie Deliverance, you know, you get it? What do you think he did? He ran to the first mechanic he could find, fixed my car. I don't care what you charge me. The guy could have had a hat saying, you know, do we cheat him and how? Name of the gas station. You would say, oh, great name. Yep. Because massive pain. That's what massive pain does to someone. And what's technically happening here, what you need to understand is that the impact the pain has is it causes the fulcrum to slide over to this side of the equation. And once it makes that slide, guess what? All of a sudden by sheer physics and gravity, all of those major negatives, one second, you take a sip, all of those major negatives, they lose their power, they lose their force. The force they exert on the scale is diminished by a factor of like, you know, 10 or 1000 depending on how far it slides over some magnitude it's reduced by. And suddenly each little positive has a massive influence on the situation. Nothing lowers a person's action threshold more than pain. Now that being said, remember, you know, when you're gathering intelligence from someone, right, you need to be essentially digging out pain during that process. You have to do that because there's two reasons why. There's really, there's two places where we really want to really amplify pain and identify it. First identify the name. All right. Initially it's during your intelligence gathering. After you've asked your noninvasive questions and you start getting down some of your more invasive ones, they could be big picture. Like, you know, what's, you know, it could start like, you know, what's, you know, what's the one thing about this current service that you have that you're not really liking when it's not doing it for you and that might elicit. Well, it's causing this breath and you might hit a pain point and they say, well, really tell me more about that. Oh my God. Oh, wow. So tell me and then you and then what you want to do there is you want to start digging in with specific more invasive questions that essentially amplify or at least bring out that pain point into the open. You bring it to the surface. Now that's very important for a couple. Here's number one, that when someone is feeling pain and you're presenting your solution, they're listening to you. They're hanging on you everywhere because they're listening from the perspective of someone who really is feeling uncomfortable and just wants to feel better. Think about that. Like when you go to the doctor and you're, if you're really sick, just talk to us, please, what do I got to do versus your annual checkup? You're like, all right, well, you know, yeah, go take these fucking Omega three fucking bills. Maybe, maybe not versus you're about to fucking have your appendix first. Doc, please cut me open and take out my appendix. I'm in pain. I can't function. So by our nature that by, by when we have a pain that's really out in the open and someone presents a solution to us, we're like, okay, I'll do it. So when you gather intelligence, if you go too quickly and don't take the time to really figure it out where their pain or uncomfortability lies, they're, they're feeling of being out of control. They're a lap. Where does that lie? What does it really mean to them? And then kind of dig into amplify by asking more questions. What will happen is if you, or even worse, by the way, sometimes you'll see a rookie salesperson that they'll, they've, they've read, they've done some straight line training and they've heard about this stuff. They'll dig out a pain point. They'll be, oh, oh, guess what? My project is perfect. Don't even, Bill, calm down now. Rest assured. I got you covered. What happens when you do that? He's like, all right, well, I really, he's just feeling good again. And then when you all for your solution, remember the syntax, right? You gather intelligence and bill report at the same time and you do your transition, then you all for your solution comes after. So now, because you jumped the gun here and try to tell them, oh, how wonderful. Don't worry, please, don't feel any angst. Please stop worrying. You got me. Please, you now listen to your solution with a far less, let's say, a far more measured eye. He's no longer like, what is it? I want to, you get it? You've taken him out of pain. That's why you do not resolve pain in your qualifying session. You just don't do that. You want to amplify pain. Say, uh-huh, you take mental notes. The place you go about resolving that pain is when you actually make your presentation. This way, when you say, well, Jim, John, Jill, Jose, Mick, Wang, Wing, Wang, you're based on everything you just said to me. This is a perfect fit for you. Let me tell you why. And now they're like, let me hear and they're hanging on your every word and they're judging everything you say, every feature, every benefit, the value proposition, the cost of benefit ratio, everything. How it measures against the competition. They're judging all of that. Every last bit of it to feeling pain versus they're listening to you like feeling good, feeling strong, feeling as what? A buyer in, that's why you're called a buyer in power. They feel power. They're in power versus a buyer in heat. They feel urgency. They got to take action now. They got to resolve a pain point. That's the difference. So you don't want to resolve pain as you qualify. You want to amplify them and say, well, Jim, let me say this based on everything you just said to me. Wow. This is the perfect, this is right up your eye. Let me tell you exactly why. And then you make your presentation. So that's the first spot is when you basically are gathering, I told you how to dig out that pain point. Okay. Now, when the second spot, well, the next, now, let me just stop. Now remember, when I told that spots on the line. I'm talking about places where we're predominantly doing one thing. You're always doing all things at once, meaning you're always trying to stay in rapport. You're always taking control of the sale. You're always asking more questions through this. And this, I just don't, never be too rigid in the application of the straight line or else it falls apart. But the point is there are all these spots where we're predominantly doing a certain thing. So when we gather and tell, one of the things that we're doing in a major way, we're looking to dig out pain and amplify that pain. Remember, when I say amplify it, people are often in denial. In other words, the way pain works, right, is what it hurts, right? And makes us feel like shit. It's designed to do that pain. Just as pain, bodily pain, discomfort, even emotional discomfort is an evolutionary feature biomechanically designed to help us actually live and not die. In other words, we have these warning systems. Hey, you know what? Something hurts here. I have to slow down. I have to protect this thing. I got to stop. I got to look a certain way. I'm hungry right now because it makes, and it's this, it's not, I'm uncomfortably hungry, which means what? Stop doing what you're doing and go eat some food. I'm dying of thirst means what? I got to drink some water because of it or else I'll die. These are all designed to allow you to live and function healthy in the world. The same thing is true of the pain that you feel emotionally, except when it gets debilitating, like it's depression, and it's something to get pain. And I've been, by the way, and I've been to this myself, right? Was in chronic pain with something in my back where you get into this pain loop and it's pain for pain sake. That's a different thing. So sometimes someone gets in chronic pain and there's a loop forms that it's no longer serving you. So the pain is not warning you of anything. You're already healed. This is very common in your body, by the way. It's a terrible thing. They've learned how to treat it more effectively. The hatch is a 1993 fucking destroyed out of fusion. It's a disaster, right? But I had pain for pain sake. Nothing structurally wrong in my body, but my brain is saying you're in pain. Great excuse to take in Quailu. It's the only upside, right? Anyway, going forward with the story, the point is, is that for the most part in the absence of a pain loop that doesn't serve anything, pain is designed to make you aware that you must do something to be well again, to feel good again. That's what it's there for. So when you amplify someone's pain, what's happening is saying you gotta act pal. You gotta act because you're not right right now. You gotta get back to feeling right again. Now what happens in that, this is very important. Why we supposed to amplify pain is that after a certain amount of time, if the pain is prolonged and you're not doing anything about it or you try to fix it and it doesn't work, guess what happens? We become numb to our own pain. We become comfortably numb as Pink Floyd would say, where we essentially ratchet down our expectations of what is good. We'll say, you know what? I'm not really in pain. This is just, this is my life. It sucks. I shouldn't be happy and joyous. You know, that's, and you'll, you have all these reasons, your brain goes, it hurts. Pain hurts. Makes you, you can't go on. So either you act on pain to resolve it or eventually guess what happens? You go into denial. The beauty of the straight line system, why it's ethical to do this is that someone's in denial, man, they live in a life of lack for no other reason that they're just in this terrible loop where they just like, they've gone to denial and they've convinced themselves as they're okay when they're really not. So by making them step back into that pain and uncomfortability, what it does, brings it back up and puts them back in reality. So that's the first place we want to identify pain in that intelligence gathering section and amplify it but not resolve it. Then now moving into the back half of the sale, right? So now let's talk about the action threshold. So we ran off first two loops. And if you know the straight line syntax, let's go through it in the back half, right? The first step in the back half is we deflect the initial objection. That's what we do, right? Then we run a loop to build more certainty and that loop always ends with you asking for the order now, which we do the second time. When you ask for the order the second time, what can happen? Well, they can either say yes, they could say not interested, very rare, right? Or hit you with another objection or they hop, right? At which point you run another loop. That is your next step. You run a second loop, right? Now you don't deflect the next objection. You have to answer that second objection head on. You run your loop. And in the back you build more certainty, except now on this loop as it closes out, you're not going to ask for the order again before you lower the action threshold. So you take the crack now, a very big crack at lowering the actual threshold by using this pattern of what's the worst that could possibly happen. You essentially hijack their strategy of running long, toxic movies and make them run a more realistic and more healthy strategy that will allow them to get something that they need. That's your next loop, right? But if they don't, sometimes by the way, you do that and it works like a magic. It really is magical, right? But it doesn't always work. Sometimes you have those really tough buys. I mean, like my father, where just like, wow, you're like, holy Christ, like, you know, that you made massive progress. You can tell that, you know, like, I can't believe this person is still not buying. What is wrong with them? Right? You're like, well, get it, right? Those are the people with this, like, you know, literally the super. So watch, here's what might happen. So here, let's visually look at this. So let's say you have like someone like my dad's ultra high action threshold person, right? So he starts off at his, his focus is almost here. It's like, it's like almost no credence given to positives and even one nasty negative is enough to plunk the scale down against you, right? Now you run this action threshold lowering pattern in your previous loop. Now just remember, let's just be clear what's what, when you're moving someone down the straight line, all the things you're saying and doing, you're not changing his action threshold yet. That's knocking off negatives and building on positives, right? Your previous patterns are about knocking out negatives and building positives. And you're not hoping to tip the scale like that. Now in the second, the second loop, now you're addressing the folk, the position of the folk room. And let's say that my dad, you ran that pattern and it moved the folk room to here, moved it to be one or two notches down. But let's say that's still the cause of, of any, maybe he had a real couple of nasty negatives still, or maybe really was this emotionally serious. It doesn't matter. You, it's still not enough. So those situations, people like that, that's when adding on pain, using the pain threshold, that's when the magic happens right there. So in other words, you've now been through your second loop and you've lowered the action threshold using words and future pacing, still not quite there yet. That's when you want to run a, and you'll get hit with an objection of some sort. All right. And you're going to loop back one more time, build certainty again, because there's going to be certain de-wains. You have to reestablish certainty again, because certainty will not last very long. It lasts for maybe a few minutes. It's just a deteriorate. This is how human beings are built, right? So you button up certainty in this loop here and your third loop now, you're going to future pay someone, okay, lowest action threshold. And then you'll say, let me ask you a question. You know, when we spoke before, you mentioned, you know, you mentioned earlier that and you go back to the beginning of the sale when you were gathering intelligence and you re-insinuate that pain point, make them step back into that pain point and start making them talk about it by asking you open-ended question. And let me just tell you one thing here, bottom line, people love to talk about their own fucking pain. It's a human thing. It's not that one person's better, good. It's just human beings. It's how we're built. We love to talk about our tale of woe, expound on how bad things are. We love to pick at our own scabs, so to speak. That's how human beings are built. So it's really not a very difficult thing to get someone talking about what bothers them, what ails them, what aches them. Just got to say, tell me about it. With that, now remember, if you are not in rapport, they will not do it. But if you're in very tight rapport and you ask that question, the rest of them, you know, you spoke with, tell me what that, you know, what do you see yourself in two years? How much has it really impacted your life? And now, oh, people, they just trust, they will start, they will go on and on digging out and as they're doing that, you're like, uh-huh. And you're using your active listening to say, I care, understand you, I feel your, you get it, I feel your pain. That's what's happening. And as you're doing that, as they start talking, guess what's happening? They start feeling more and more pain. They start, they start stepping into their own pain. And as they do that, the action threshold starts to drift naturally to the right. So after you've done this and you've had them literally, you know, really step into their pain like that, right? Then what you want to do is say, I totally get that. In fact, let me say this and you want to match the straight line system. Okay. To what they can say, but first amplify. So I believe in like 10 minutes to the airport, right? And then I come on through the one day turnaround back in and I go to Dublin next week, right? But we'll do the Q&A on Sunday. I don't know when, but the point is, is that when you do this correctly, it really is you're very much like one of those, you know, old fashioned safe crackers who puts their ear to this tumbler. You know, you spin it to the right and you get that first click and you know, you got that first number in the buying combination or the safes combination, right? But you don't pull down the handle. You don't try to open the safe because you're aware that the safest got more than one number, right? It's just why why would you do it? You wouldn't do it. It doesn't make sense, right? Same thing goes when you then spin the number in the, the tumble in the opposite direction. You click, you get a second number, right? You got the first two numbers locked down tight. They must love your product. They must trust you. That's your numbers with, with human influence, right? But would you ask for the order? No, why? Because you know that every safe, even the ones that aren't built that well, like the low action, threshold, easy ones that easy ones to crack open, they always have three numbers, right? The fucking master locks got three numbers, right? The most simple ones have three because that's the outside. It just be too easy to do. Do a, you can guess them by just trying all the combinations. Three becomes difficult, right? So you spin it in the opposite direction. You hear that third click. What do you do now? You pull down on the handle and try to open the safe. Sometimes it opens. It opens when you have somebody with a very low action threshold, like me. Why? Because yeah, I still have two numbers left. You don't need to work with me. You got enough. You've done enough. I'm positive enough. I'm certain enough in the first three I can buy. You'll never have to address my fourth and fifth number. Sometimes you might, but not in this situation. But if you try to pull down on the handle, what does it mean? Well, it means one of two things. Either A, you didn't get one of these first three numbers, right? But also you put more likely, if you were listening carefully, it means, ah, I've got a bit more of a complex safe here. I've got a fourth number, probably a fifth one, too. So you're going to start from the beginning again. You're going to loop back. Make sure you're going to run your pants to shore up the first 10, shore up the second 10, shore up the third 10. You've got to make sure those three numbers go certainty ways. So you're spinning back on them, get those first three. Now you spin again for the fourth one. Click. That's when you lower the action threshold, when you expand that pattern. You click it, now you try again. And by the way, very often you'll find most safes have four numbers in their combination, the safe of the human mind for buying. Some don't. Some are like my dad. You got to go to that fifth number so you'll start again. You'll loop back. You'll shore up your first four numbers. Make sure you've got them locked down tight. When you hit that fifth number, you add on pain and boom. That's when that handle will most assuredly open or not. And when I say not means because not everyone's closeable. That's just the bottom line. You cannot close everyone. It's a fallacy. It's fiction. Anyone who tells you otherwise is a fucking liar. The point I'm making when I say you'll close anyone who's closeable, what I mean is that you won't walk away from a sales encounter saying, damn, you know, I wish you were here. Gordon was there or another top, top producers. He would have gotten them. No, you'll feel like that yourself. You'll say, you know what, he didn't say yes, but no one could have closed them. They weren't closeable. That's how you want to feel. And when you feel that way, it will give you the emotional fortitude, the wherewithal, to basically knock on as many doors as you have to, dial the phone as many times as it takes, do whatever leads, research, whatever it takes. Because you know that when you get in front of somebody and you can make an actual presentation, someone who's qualified, you're going to close with its possible. That's power.
